title: Anomaly Detection: Spotting the Odd One Out
concept: Anomaly detection finds data points that break the pattern the rest of the data follows, powering tools like fraud alerts and manufacturing defect scanners.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does dimensionality reduction do?

options.text: Compresses data with many features down to the few that carry the most signal
Groups similar data points into clusters
Predicts a category label for each data point

exercises.explain: Right! Dimensionality reduction simplifies data down to the features that matter most.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Clustering, dimensionality reduction, and anomaly detection are the three unsupervised tasks you've learned this unit.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Anomaly detection is the third piece — today's lesson.

cards.title: The clown at the business meeting
cards.body: If everyone in a room is wearing a suit and one person walks in wearing a full clown costume, you don't need a rulebook to know something's different — the contrast with everyone else is the giveaway.

cards.title: What is anomaly detection?
cards.body: Anomaly detection is an unsupervised task that finds data points that don't fit the pattern the rest of the data follows. It doesn't need labels marking something as "weird" — it learns what "normal" looks like from the data itself, then flags anything that stands out.

cards.title: The one text that doesn't fit
cards.body: You text the same five friends every day, at roughly the same times, using the same handful of words. Then one day a message goes out at 3am to a number you've never contacted, saying something totally unlike anything you'd normally write. Even without being told, you can sense something's off — that's the same contrast anomaly detection looks for in data.

cards.title: Fraud and factory defects
cards.body: Fraud detection: a credit card is normally used at the same few stores in one city — a charge in a different country ten minutes later doesn't fit the pattern and gets flagged. Manufacturing defects: a factory camera scans thousands of identical parts rolling by, and a scratched or misshapen one immediately stands out from the rest.

cards.title: It's not just about spotting "bad" data
cards.body: People assume anomaly detection only catches errors or fraud. In reality, it just flags anything statistically unusual — sometimes that's fraud or a defect, but sometimes it's a rare, perfectly valid event, like an unusually large but legitimate purchase. A human usually decides what to do next.

cards.title: Normal pattern vs. the outlier
cards.body: What anomaly detection is looking for:

left.text:
Most data points cluster together
Follows a predictable shape

right.text:
Sits far from the rest
Breaks the expected pattern

exercises.prompt: A bank flags one transaction that looks nothing like a customer's usual spending pattern. Which unsupervised task is this?

options.text: Anomaly detection
Clustering
Dimensionality reduction

exercises.explain: Right! Flagging a single data point that breaks the usual pattern is anomaly detection.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Anomaly detection needs labeled examples of "fraud" and "not fraud" before it can flag anything unusual.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Anomaly detection learns what "normal" looks like from the data itself, with no labels required.

exercises.sentence: Anomaly detection flags data points that don't fit the ___ the rest of the data follows.
exercises.explain: Anomaly detection compares each point to the pattern the rest of the data follows and flags what breaks it.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the best example of anomaly detection?

options.text: A security camera system flagging one shopper whose movement pattern looks nothing like typical browsing behavior
A store discovering three natural groups of shoppers based on buying habits
A model predicting the exact number of items a shopper will buy

exercises.explain: Correct! Flagging one point that breaks the expected pattern, with no predefined label, is anomaly detection.

exercises.prompt: A factory's quality-control camera scans 10,000 identical bottles per hour and flags the rare one with a cracked cap. What made this anomaly detection rather than clustering?

options.text: The goal was finding the one point that breaks the pattern, not sorting everything into groups
It involved a factory, and factories always use anomaly detection
It used a camera instead of numbers
Clustering can't be used on physical objects

exercises.explain: Exactly! Clustering sorts everything into groups where every item belongs somewhere. Anomaly detection specifically hunts for the item that doesn't belong.

exercises.prompt: An airline monitors engine sensor readings from thousands of flights. Almost all readings cluster in a tight, predictable range, but one engine's readings drift far outside that range over a single flight. A teammate says, "Let's build a classifier trained on 'broken engine' vs 'healthy engine' examples." Why might anomaly detection be the better first step?

options.text: There likely aren't enough labeled "broken engine" examples to train a classifier, but anomaly detection can flag unusual readings just by learning what normal engine behavior looks like
Classification is always better than anomaly detection for safety-critical systems
Anomaly detection requires engine failure labels to work, just like classification does
Sensor data can only be analyzed with dimensionality reduction

exercises.explain: Exactly! Engine failures are rare, so labeled failure examples are scarce. Anomaly detection sidesteps that by learning normal behavior and flagging deviations, without needing failure labels.

exercises.prompt: An online store's anomaly detection system flags a customer's $2,000 purchase as unusual, since that customer normally spends under $50. It turns out the customer was legitimately buying a gift for a wedding. Was the anomaly detection system wrong to flag it?

options.text: No — it correctly identified a statistically unusual pattern; anomaly detection flags what's different, and a human still decides whether it's fraud or a valid rare event
Yes — anomaly detection should only ever flag actual fraud
Yes — a correctly working system would never flag a real customer
No, but only because wedding gifts are always flagged automatically

exercises.explain: Exactly! Anomaly detection flags anything that breaks the usual pattern — it doesn't know "why." Deciding whether a flagged event is fraud or just a rare, valid one is a judgment call made afterward.
